author: @zer0nix
text:
For even more efficiency and quiet, the trailing edge should also be serrated, like an owls wing! They're finally beginning to incorporate that design into jetliners due to noise and efficiency regulations, despite the fact that incorporating them before would have saved hundreds of millions of dollars in fuel costs...

I remember reading about the bumpy fins back in the 90s and I'm still surprised that I don't see that design in more places, especially with injection molded or 3d printed parts! I guess no one has discovered what size of bumps is the most efficient, and no one had bothered to do a real world head to head comparison to see just how much more efficient they are!

I think I read also that a rough surface on turbine blades also increases efficiency at lower wind speeds by catching the wind better, but a quick Google search reveals that this may already have been incorporated invisibly, and the roughness is only on one side, with the other side being smooth to reduce drag and generate lift. Still, bumps don't have to be rough and seem to concentrate the force of the wind, in addition to being a stronger and more flexible shape than flat plates, thus requiring less printed material per surface area! This is a super exciting direction to go in since a real world comparison shows how much more efficient these nature inspired designs are, yet no one seems to be using them!

text: Tubercles were tried on cars, even F1 cars, back in the 1970s. The effect was positive at speeds below about 40mph and increasingly negative above that. This suggests to me that the benefit for wind turbines is likely to be for VAWTs and very small HAWTs.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I live in a very deep valley where the wind washes over the peaks of the mountains and skips us entirely.

If the wind at the peak is 3 meters per second, you can hold a smoldering tip [like an incense stick] and it won't even waft from side to side.

When one stands at the highest point on my property, one can see trees in a full 360 degrees at less than 166 meters in all directions, and a height over 22 meters at the lowest point.

And, on top of that, I have yet to pull any of the motors out of any of the free printers I have been given.

Therefore...

It could still yet be a while before I can get you those results.

While I do have a fan I set out specifically to cool off the area where my goats live, it will be warm weather again before I start that thing up, as in this weather one will get severely frost bitten standing in front of it!

I was just suggesting that for those who would want to know what the torque difference is.

Personally, where I live, unless I can buy up some land where there is a strong prevailing wind, there is really no reason for me to worry about wind energy.

Living in a box canyon with only 3.2 acres on the leeward side of the valley really leaves you with nothing to work with.

Most of my energy research is in biofuels. [ethanol, methanol, butanol, and natural gas (natural gas from goat manure, since I raise them)]

author: @antoniiocaluso1071
text: built a catamaran a decade ago, and built her twin rudders with "tubercles" (silly name, yes?) on  the leading edge of each. BUT...instead of protruding them forward, as is "typical" (no disrespect to humpback whales intended), they were faired INTO each's leading edge, along it's vertical length. Seems to increase the "directionality" of the waterflow, & increasing the trailing "tail". Reduced "rudder chatter" at speed, too. Sometimes, for we "olde guys", its never too-late to build oneself a fine water craft!! :-) btw...my cat's design is called "ECO6", and its plans are very-affordable. Bernd Kohler its Designer, and notable for his innovative designs (some "fly"!!). Shhh...I haven't told him of that modification (as my others) as you know how "incendiary" some Franco-Dutch geniuses can become! :-))))

author: @gshingles
text:
A slight problem with your experimental setup is that your body is part of the experiment but if you're confident your observations weren't affected by that then it looks pretty clear cut. Personally I don't think it has as much to do with increased lift as it does with reducing drag. On a continuous blade contour the air has to depart 'all at once' where there might be interference from fast air going to stationary air, so it might cause turbulence in the area trailing the blade. The nodules might promote the departure of the laminar flow in distinct regions, maybe even in vortices, which could separate the areas of turbulence; a vortex would be able to extend away from the blade for quite a distance before turbulence formed.
In fact, I've wondered for a while if drone props were made with this feature whether they would be a lot quieter. Maybe they would just get a lot more whiny? :)

author: @davidl.howser9707
text: Hi Robert, Maybe test performance when modifying both the leading, and trailing edge skin textures of the sycamore samara seed. To the leading edge add the tubercle bumps copied from humpback whale's fin leading edge ( Done). Next.....to the trailing edge add the owl's wing trailing very fine comb texture to spread out the air foils trailing edge wake leaving the foil. All surface texture changes are hoped to reduce noise, and create more lift with reduced turbulence experienced by the next in line rotating / trailing blade cutting through a much less  disturbed air molecule fluid stream. Thank You Robert for being you….intelligent, perceptive, intuitive, genuine, passionate, humble, fun, and thought provoking ! Enjoy each Day. Please send a "Hello" to Luke coming from here to there from across the pond. Robert, you are most worthy of the "The Global Teacher Prize".

replies.author: @trevorgoddard2278
replies.text:
Unfortunately fine feather like structures and 3D printing really don't go well together.
It might be possible with custom written Gcode, but the time and effort required to write it (or more likely write the software to produce it) is almost certainly not worth it for the limited gain it would be likely to produce, as well as requiring a skilled programmer to write it.

replies.author: @SaltGrains_Fready
replies.text: ALL he needs is a small dual tripod with a rod connecting them horizontally 2 meters high. Each unit can mount on the cross bar equally  spaced in from the end with an anemometer in the middle. That gives uniform flow and the value of the flow. Simple  The wind over the hill in the open is more uniform than any home brew wind tunnel will ever be. They usually end up with the turbines sitting at a 30 degree angle in the flow. AND it would cost him $30K quid and take up a long area and require a high horsepower engine and fan.

author: @MrChris20912
text:
Fascinating! Low speed, organic airfoil. With the added nodules, there's more leading edge surface area to for a fluid medium to catch, this giving thrust at lower speeds. ( think fibrules on a dandelion seed)  


Now that I think about it, there are similar underwater protuberances on the fronts of larger cargo vessels as well. Just the one large bump, rather than many small ones, but for much the same reason. (Maybe?)
